nativeRange.habitat: Found in shallow, slow-moving swamps, marshes, freshwater lagoons, and muddy river margins across much of Africa, where it tends to lurk among dense vegetation and soft substrate.

diet.notes: Primarily accepts meaty foods including frozen krill, silversides, whole prawns, earthworms, bloodworms, and brine shrimp. Large sinking pellets are also accepted by many individuals. Hunts by scent rather than sight — poor vision means target feeding is often needed to ensure the fish locates food, especially in a community tank where faster-moving tankmates can intercept meals.

avoidWith: fish smaller than the bichir's mouth gape

imageCandidates.notes: Elma; 2396x1332; tack-sharp studio profile on a black ground showing the head, row of dorsal finlets and elongated olive body — the diagnostic prehistoric bichir look.
Elma from Reykjavik; 1947x1121; two wild-type individuals on substrate in a planted aquarium, showing the faint body banding and how they are kept.
5snake5; 2761x1719; sharp head-on portrait of the prehistoric face and barbels (feature close-up, not a whole-body shot).

summary: The Senegal Bichir is a primitive, air-breathing predator from across Africa, widely kept by intermediate aquarists for its prehistoric appearance. It hunts by scent rather than sight, is most active at dusk, and poses a serious escape risk — a tight-fitting, weighted lid is essential.
careNotes:
LIVING FOSSIL: Polypterus senegalus has Cretaceous origins and is one of the most primitive ray-finned fishes kept in the hobby. It possesses a paired, lung-like swim bladder that allows facultative air-breathing at the water surface; it can survive briefly out of water when moist, but may actually drown if denied access to atmospheric air — a small gap at the water surface must always be maintained.

ESCAPE RISK: This species is an accomplished escape artist. Its eel-like body can pass through surprisingly small gaps; a tight-fitting, weighted lid and covered or screened filter intakes are mandatory. It can survive short excursions on land due to its air-breathing ability.

FEEDING: Hunts by olfaction rather than vision — competition at feeding time with faster-sighted tankmates is a real concern. Target feeding with tongs or a feeding stick is strongly advisable. Crepuscular activity peak means evening feedings tend to be most productive. Most specimens will transition from live/fresh food to frozen or large sinking pellets with patience.

COMPATIBILITY: Generally docile toward fish it cannot fit in its mouth, but will consume any tankmate small enough to swallow. Any fish smaller than roughly one-third the bichir's body length is at risk. Conspecific pairs or groups are possible in a sufficiently large tank (500+ L) with multiple hiding spots; two bichirs of mismatched size can result in the smaller being eaten.

SIZE: Captive adults typically reach 30–45 cm. Wild specimens have been recorded up to 70 cm, but this is rarely seen in home aquaria.

COLOR VARIANTS: Several color forms are available in the trade: wild-type (grey-green with banding), Albino (pink/yellow), and Platinum (near-white). All are the same species and have identical care requirements.

TANK: A long footprint (≥150 cm) is more important than depth. Smooth substrate (fine sand or smooth gravel) protects the belly. Subdued lighting, dense hiding spots (driftwood, PVC pipe, caves), and low flow suit this species. Hardy and forgiving of a wide range of water parameters within the stated ranges.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is extremely rarely documented and has generally not been achieved reliably by hobbyists. In the wild, spawning occurs during the rainy season and is likely triggered by temperature drops and changes in water chemistry (softer, more acidic water). Eggs are scattered among vegetation; hatching occurs in roughly 3–4 days; fry become free-swimming within a few days of hatching. Fry have external feathery gills in early life. Breeding attempts in captivity are strongly associated with specialist facilities rather than home aquaria.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (stated ~35 cm captive / 90 gal tank), temperature (22–28°C), pH (6.5–7.5), temperament, and diet. Minor disagreements: tank size stated as ~340 L vs SeriouslyFish 540 L — biased toward larger per playbook. Adult size agrees well at captive range. Lifespan 15–20 yr vs SF 30+ yr; averaged to 15–30 yr range.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Polypteriformes), maximum wild size (70 cm SL), expanded native range (West/Central/East Africa; Nile, Volta, Niger, Chad, Congo basins), and habitat prose. Wild max size of 70 cm documented in careNotes.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely if ever achieved in home aquaria. A rainy-season trigger is suspected, and fry carry external feathery gills in early life.
